coredata.dc:title: A magnetic nanocomposite optosensing probe based on porous graphene, selective polymer and quantum dots for the detection of cefoperazone in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Chaitong, Nuntanut
Chansud, Nutnicha
Orachorn, Naphatsakorn
Limbut, Warakorn
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: An optosensor based on synthesized magnetic composite sensing probes was designed to detect ultra-trace levels of cefoperazone. The nanocomposite probe integrated porous graphene, quantum dots, magnetite nanoparticles and highly specific polymer. These substances produced an ultra-sensitive, selective and rapid sensor. The fluorescence emission of the nanoprobes was quenched when cefoperazone interacted with the imprinted binding sites. The developed probe exhibited good linearity toward cefoperazone from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.09 μg L−1. The fabricated magnetic nanocomposite sensor showed good specificity for cefoperazone with an imprinting factor of 7.5 and was not interfered by the analog structures ceftriaxone, cefazolin, cefradine, ceftazidime and cefalexin. The nanocomposite probe successfully detected cefoperazone in milk. Recoveries ranged from 85.3 to 98.3% with RSDs < 6%. Analysis results agreed well with HPLC but the advantages of the optosensor include a simple, rapid procedure, cost-effective equipment and environmental friendliness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous graphene
Quantum dots
Optosensor
Fluorescence
Cefoperazone
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the magnetic nanocomposite probe
Characterization of QDs and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe
Optimization of the probe fabrication and detection condition
Effect of porous graphene loading on probe sensitivity
Effect of pH of dispersion solution
Effect of TM to MN to CL ratio
Comparison of the nanooptosensor with other works

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Research Council of Thailand ( NRCT5-RSA63022-03 ). The authors would like to acknowledge the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The diagram illustrates the fabrication procedure of a magnetic nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe for cefoperazone detection.

The micrographs are an SEM image of porous graphene (A), a TEM image of a TGA capped CdTe QD (B) and an SEM image of the synthesized magnetic nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe (C). The histogram (D) shows the size distribution of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the magnetic nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe (A) and the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP nanoprobe (B) were produced in the presence of different concentrations of cefoperazone. The calibration curves (C) were plotted for the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe (a) and the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probe (b). The photograph (D) shows the composite probe under UV light without (left) and with (right) cefaperazone.

Comparison of the determination of cefoperazone in spiked milk samples using the magnetic composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe and HPLC (A). HPLC chromatograms were produced from milk samples spiked with cefoperazone at 0.25 (a), 0.5 (b), 1.0 (c) and 2.5 mg L−1 (d).
Cefoperazone in milk was determined using the nano-optosensor based on a magnetic composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe (n = 5).
A comparison of analytical methods for the detection of cefoperazone.

head.ce:title: A magnetic nanocomposite optosensing probe based on porous graphene, selective polymer and quantum dots for the detection of cefoperazone in milk

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optosensor based on synthesized magnetic composite sensing probes was designed to detect ultra-trace levels of cefoperazone. The nanocomposite probe integrated porous graphene, quantum dots, magnetite nanoparticles and highly specific polymer. These substances produced an ultra-sensitive, selective and rapid sensor. The fluorescence emission of the nanoprobes was quenched when cefoperazone interacted with the imprinted binding sites. The developed probe exhibited good linearity toward cefoperazone from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.09 μg L−1. The fabricated magnetic nanocomposite sensor showed good specificity for cefoperazone with an imprinting factor of 7.5 and was not interfered by the analog structures ceftriaxone, cefazolin, cefradine, ceftazidime and cefalexin. The nanocomposite probe successfully detected cefoperazone in milk. Recoveries ranged from 85.3 to 98.3% with RSDs < 6%. Analysis results agreed well with HPLC but the advantages of the optosensor include a simple, rapid procedure, cost-effective equipment and environmental friendliness.

ce:section.ce:para: Cefoperazone is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that is extremely effective against Gram negative bacteria and is normally utilized for the treatment of sepsis [1]. The determination of cefoperazone in food samples is required for reasons of food safety and although several methods can be used to detect cefoperazone, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is widely used due to its good sensitivity and precision [2]. However, HPLC requires long analysis time, expensive and complicated equipment, and uses large volumes of toxic solvents. These problems can be avoided using the fluorescence quenching of optical probes to detect trace organic compounds. The technique, known as spectrofluorimetry, is not only simple, rapid and cost-effective but also environmentally friendly since samples are analysed as small volumes of aqueous solution without toxic solvent [3]. The sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescent probes can be enhanced by fabricating nanocomposite probes that use materials with good optical properties and high specificity. Among the currently available optical materials for the fabrication of nanocomposite probes, quantum dots provide high intensity emissions, good chemical stability and are easy to produce [4].

Composite fluorescence probes have exhibited high sensitivity toward some organic compounds but for the detection of trace or ultra-trace target analytes in complex samples which contain matric interferences, better selectivity or specificity is generally required. The selectivity of a composite probe can be improved using molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIP is a highly specific material which can be prepared using the target analyte as a template molecule in the presence of a monomer and cross linker. After polymerization is complete the template is removed and specific recognition cavities are created [5]. The formed cavities are specific to the target analyte in size and shape and are receptive to specific functional groups, which can rebind for analyte detection. MIP has good stability, is easy to produce and it has been utilized in applications such as solid phase extraction [6,7], electrode modification for electrochemical sensors [8], biosensors [9] and optical nanosensors [10]. To enhance binding between a target analyte and the nanoprobe, high affinity materials with large surface areas can be incorporated into MIP. Porous graphene (PGr) is a good choice of high affinity material for the adsorption of cefoperazone since it has a large surface area which can adsorb the molecule via π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding [11]. Improvements in sensitivity and selectivity can be reinforced by pre-concentration and enrichment of the target analyte. This approach will help to improve the limit of detection to enable the determination of trace or ultra-trace analyte levels. A simple and convenient enrichment procedure can be realized by incorporating magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) into a composite probe. The entrapment of magnetite nanoparticles in composite materials was reported in the fabrication of a dispersive magnetic solid phase extraction sorbent [12] and a magnetic nanocomposite optosensing probe for cefoperazone analysis could equally be developed.

In this work, a composite optosensing probe was fabricated by incorporating PGr, quantum dots (QDs) and Fe3O4 into MIP (PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP). The fabrication condition of the magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe was optimized and its analytical performance was investigated. The fabricated optosensing probe was then characterized and applied to detect cefoperazone in milk samples. Its accuracy was determined by analysis of spiked milk samples and the analysis results were compared with HPLC analysis.

Cefoperazone and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Thioglycolic acid (TGA), sodium borohydride, cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2H2O), tellurium (99.8%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES ≥ 98%), FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O were from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Porous graphene (PGr) (C2131121D3) was from Gwent Group Ltd., (Mamhilad, Pontypool, UK). Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O were purchased from Univar Chemical (Washington, USA). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethanol and ammonium solution (NH4OH) were purchased from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

Fluorescence intensity was measured with an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorophotometer (Agilent Technologies) and UV–Vis spectra were recorded with an Avaspec 2048 spectrometer (Apeldoorn, Netherlands). The FT-IR spectra were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphology of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP was observed using a JEM-5200 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL, Japan). TEM image was obtained from the Philips TECNAI 20 (Eindhoven, the Netherlands). Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were obtained using ASAP 2460 Micromeristic, USA. Detail of HPLC condition is given in the Supplementary material.

The quantum dot nanoparticles were synthesized as described in a previous report [13]. Briefly, 46 mg of CdCl2 were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water (DI) and 30 μL of TGA were mixed into the CdCl2 solution. The pH of the mixture solution was adjusted to 11.5 with NaOH (1.0 M) and refluxed under N2 at 90 °C using a three-necked flask. Meanwhile, NaHTe solution was prepared by mixing 45.5 mg of NaBH4 and 50.8 mg of Te powder in 2.0 mL of DI water. Then, 0.50 mL of the NaHTe solution was rapidly injected into the CdCl2 and TGA mixture solution and refluxed for 10 min. An orange solution was obtained and synthesized TGA capped cadmium telluride (CdTe) QD nanoparticles were precipitated with 25 mL of ethanol.

Magnetite nanoparticles were synthesized following a method described in a previous work [14]. Briefly, 9.4 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 3.4 g of FeCl2·4H2O were mixed in 160 mL of DI water, transferred into a round-bottomed flask and refluxed under N2 at 80 °C for 10 min. Then, 20 mL of NH4OH were added to the solution and reflux continued for 1 h to obtain synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which were separated using an external magnet, washed twice with 80 mL of DI water and then dried at 70 °C for 6 h. To embed the Fe3O4 nanoparticles in silica, a mixture solution was prepared containing 100 mL of ethanol, 50 mL of DI water and 2 mL of NH4OH. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were added to the mixture, 2.0 mL of TEOS were introduced, and the mixture was stirred at 300 rpm for 12 h. Finally, the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2 were isolated from the solution, washed with 20 mL DI water and dried at 70 °C for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the magnetic nanocomposite probe
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the nanocomposite sensing probe, 16.10 mg of cefoperazone were dissolved in 5.0 mL of DI water and 36 μL of APTES were added to the solution, which was stirred at 500 rpm for 1 h. Then, 5.0 mL of CdTe QDs and 5.0 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 were added and the mixture solution was continuously stirred for 1 h. After stirring, 120 μL of TEOS, 150 μL of NH4OH and 2.5 mg of porous graphene were added. Stirring continued at 500 rpm for 4 h at which point the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was obtained. The template was removed by washing with methanol until spectrophotometry could no longer detect a signal of the template molecule in the washing solution. A magnetic nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probe was also fabricated using the same procedure but without the cefoperazone template. The synthesis procedure of the magnetic nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence was measured in 2.0 mL of standard cefoperazone or sample solution. 200 μL of composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe (1.0 mg mL−1) was added to the sample and the mixture was incubated under rotation for 30 min to allow binding to occur between cefoperazone and recognition sites on the surface of the nanoprobe. After 30 min, the nanoprobe was isolated using a magnet and was dispersed in 200 μL of phosphate buffer at pH 7.0. The fluorescence emission of the dispersed nanoprobe was measured between 400 and 700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm.

Milk samples were pre-treated as follows: 10 mL of milk sample were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove fat. To precipitate proteins, 10 mL of acetonitrile were added and the sample was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was separated and evaporated at 60 °C. The residue was dissolved in 2.0 mL of DI water and the solution was then analysed to detect cefoperazone using an optosensing system based on the fabricated composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of QDs and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of TGA-capped CdTe QDs was determined from the maximum absorption wavelength at 501 nm as described in previous work [4]. The particle size was about 2.37 nm. The peak of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe QDs (Fig. S1) was narrow, symmetrical and exhibited high fluorescence intensity, which is suitable for sensing applications.

An SEM image of porous graphene and a TEM image of a TGA-capped CdTe QD are showed in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. The TEM image exhibited the particles size agreed with those calculated. An SEM image of the composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe is showed in Fig. 2C. The synthesized magnetic composite probe showed a rough surface made up of uniform spherical particles with a diameter of 160–260 nm (Fig. 2D).

During the copolymerization of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe, cefoperazone, APTES, TGA-capped CdTe QDs and PGr first self-assembled via π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding. When the TEOS cross-linker and NH4OH catalyst were introduced into the mixture, the composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoparticles were formed, entrapping the silicon-embedded Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the composite. Before removal of the cefoperazone template, the fluorescence emission intensity of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP was about half that of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP. When the template molecule was removed, the fluorescence emission intensity of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was almost the same as the intensity of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP (Fig. S2). These results implied that the magnetic composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was synthesized and the template was completely removed. The copolymerization procedure of the developed nanocomposite probe was simple to achieve under mild conditions.

The porous graphene, magnetite nanoparticles, and synthesized nanocomposite probe were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. The spectrum of porous graphene (Fig. S3a) showed characteristic peaks at 1273, 1384, 1630, 2934 and 3422 cm−1, which correspond to C–H bending, C–O stretching, CC stretching of the aromatic ring of graphene, CH2 bonding and OH stretching, respectively. In the spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fig. S3b), the absorption peaks at 472, 592, 1061 and 3422 cm−1 are related to Si-O stretching, Fe-O-Fe stretching, the Si-O-Si stretching of silica and OH stretching, respectively. The spectrum of magnetic PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP (Fig. S3c) presented an additional peak at 1579 cm−1 which corresponded to CO stretching. The FT-IR results confirmed that the composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe was successfully synthesized and contained specific recognition cavities for the adsorption of cefoperazone. The FT-IR spectrum of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP was not significantly different from the FT-IR spectrum of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP due to their similar compositions (Fig. S3d).

The quantum yield (QY) was determined using Rhodamine 6G as the standard. The QYs of CdTe QDs and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP were 0.90 and 0.60, respectively. The BET specific surface areas of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP were 20.160 m2 g−1 and 16.010 m2 g−1, respectively. The surface area of the magnetic composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe was greater because it contained specific recognition cavities for cefoperazone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the probe fabrication and detection condition
ce:section.ce:para: Several parameters of the fabrication of the nanocomposite probe and the detection condition could affect the sensitivity of the system toward cefoperazone. To optimize the fabrication and detection conditions, the effects of probe incubation time, porous graphene loading, dispersion solution pH, and template molecule (TM) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL) ratio were investigated. The sensitivity of nanocomposite probes for the detection of cefoperazone was calculated by plotting calibration curve of fluorescence intensity change (F0/F) and concentration of cefoperazone in the range of 0.5 to 10 μg L−1. The slope of calibration curve indicated the sensitivity of the developed optosensor.

The appropriate binding time between the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe and cefoperazone was evaluated by incubating the two together for 60 min (Fig. S4). Quenching efficiency (F0/F) increased from 2 min to 30 min and then remained constant to 60 min. Thus, the incubation time of 30 min was chosen for the pre-concentration and detection of cefoperazone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of porous graphene loading on probe sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of porous graphene used in the fabrication of the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was varied from 0.0 mg to 20.0 mg and the effect on sensitivity toward cefoperazone was determined. The best sensitivity was achieved using a probe fabricated with 2.5 mg of porous graphene (Fig. S5A). The lower sensitivity of composite probes fabricated with lower amounts of porous graphene may have been due to the fewer affinity adsorption sites for cefoperazone. Sensitivity was also low when the loading of porous graphene exceeded 10.0 mg. Excess porous graphene could prevent the formation of recognition sites in the MIP layer, which subsequently reduced sensitivity. Therefore, the magnetic PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe was developed further using a probe synthesized with 2.5 mg of porous graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of dispersion solution
ce:section.ce:para: The PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was dispersed in phosphate buffer at pH 5.0, 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0 and the effect of dispersion solution pH on the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching was determined. Sensitivity was highest at pH 7.0 (Fig. S5B). The interaction between the composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe and cefoperazone involves π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding. At pH 5.0 and 6.0, sensitivity was lower due to protonation of the monomer (APTES), which reduced binding with cefoperazone. Sensitivity at pH 8.0 was lower perhaps because silica layers of the MIP were destroyed in the alkaline condition and the binding sites of the probes were disrupted. Thus, buffer solution at pH 7.0 was chosen to disperse the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe for cefoperazone detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of TM to MN to CL ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedure of the magnetic PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was optimized by varying the mole ratios of TM to MN and CL. The effect of the ratio of TM: MN was investigated at 1:4, 1:6 and 1:8, and the effect of the ratio of TM:CL was studied at 1:15, 1:20 and 1:30. The mole ratio of TM:MN:CL of 1:6:20 provided the highest sensitivity toward cefoperazone (Fig. S5C). Lower sensitivity was obtained with the smallest amount of MN (1:4) because of the low number of NH2 groups in the probe. Sensitivity was also lower when the largest amount of MN was used (1:8). This result was affected by excess MN, which inhibited the binding of cefoperazone and obstructed recognition sites. The mole ratio of TM to CL also affected sensitivity toward cefoperazone. Sensitivity was highest at the ratio of 1:20 and lowest at the ratio of 1:30 (Fig. S5D). The highest amount of CL (1:30) produced a probe of low sensitivity probably because excess CL hindered the movement of the MN, interfering with its binding to the TM and resulting in a low number of recognition sites in the MIP. Sensitivity was also rather low at the ratio of 1:15 because, at this level of CL, the physical structure of MIP was weak and the formation of recognition sites less efficient. Thus, a ratio of TM:MN:CL of 1:6:20 was chosen for the fabrication of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe.

Different composite nanoprobes were evaluated for their sensitivity toward cefoperazone detection. The nanoprobes were composites of CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP, PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP, CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP. CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP exhibited the lowest sensitivity toward cefoperazone due to the absence of specific binding sites. The PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe provided the highest sensitivity due to PGr enhanced the adsorption of cefoperazone on the nanoprobe via π- π interaction, and MIP enhanced specific binding (Fig. S6).

The magnetic composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP and PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probes were used to determine cefoperazone under the optimal conditions. The linearity of response of the nano-optosensors was investigated by determining a series of different concentrations of cefoperazone. The emission intensity of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe decreased according to the concentration of cefoperazone (Fig. 3 A). The emission intensity of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP nanoprobe also decreased with the concentration of cefoperazone but each loss of intensity was less pronounced (Fig. 3B). These slight decreases in emission intensity with increments of cefoperazone concentration were due to the nonspecific adsorption of the target molecule on the non-imprinted nanoprobe. The magnetic PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe exhibited a good linear range for cefoperazone detection from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 3C). Calculated in accordance with the IUPAC criteria, the LOD and LOQ were 0.09 and 0.32 μg L−1, respectively. The repeatability of the developed optosensor was determined in term of intra-day and inter-day precision with was performed within a day (n = 6) and among days (n = 6), respectively. The RSDs of sensitivity were 2.0% for intra-day and 2.5% for inter-day. These results demonstrated that the developed method could be applied for trace detection of cefoperazone with good repeatability

A photograph showing the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe without and with cefoperazone is shown in Fig. 3D. The specificity of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was determined from the imprinting factor (IF), which was obtained from the ratio of Ksv,MIP and Ksv,NIP. The IF was 7.5. Further, the absorption spectrum of cefoperazone did not overlap with the emission spectrum of PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe (Fig. S7). This result indicated that the quenching mechanism was not due to energy transfer [15]. In addition, cefoperazone showed an apparent absorption band overlap with the excitation spectrum of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe. Therefore, the quenching of emission in the presence of cefoperazone was caused by electron transfer from CdTe QDs when cefoperazone bound with NH2 groups of APTES. The fluorescence quenching of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP nanoprobe can be explained by the Stern-Volmer equation [16], F0/F = 1 + Ksv [C], where F0 and F are respectively the emission intensity in the absence and presence of cefoperazone, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [C] is the concentration of cefoperazone.

The selectivity of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe for the detection of cefoperazone was evaluated by applying the nanoprobe to determine structural analogs of cefoperazone, including ceftriaxone, cefazolin, cefalexin, cefradine and ceftazidime (Fig. S8). The PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probe was also tested against the same analogs. The PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was much more sensitive toward cefoperazone than toward the structural analog compounds, while the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probe exhibited similar sensitivities toward cefoperazone and the structural analogs (Fig. S9A) structures compounds. These results implied that the composite MIP contained specific binding sites for cefoperazone which were complementary in size, shape and functional groups but the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/NIP probe had no specific binding sites for cefoperazone. The specificity of nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was verified by a competitive study, which varied the ratio of cefazolin to cefoperazone (Ccefazolin/Ccefoperazone). The sensitivities were not different when the ratio of Ccefazolin/Ccefoperazone was increased (Fig. S9B). This result indicated that the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe produced binding sites that were highly specific to cefoperazone.

The stability of the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was evaluated in phosphate buffer solution pH 7.0. There was no difference in the emission intensity within 200 min (response > 90%) (Fig. S10A). This result confirmed the good stability of the nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe. The decreasing of response at longer time may due to the precipitation of the nanoprobe. The fabrication reproducibility of nanocomposite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was evaluated by synthesis different batches (n = 6) and used to detect cefoperazone (Fig. S10B). The RSD of the average sensitivity across the batches was 3.6%, which indicated the good reproducibility of the fabrication of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the nanooptosensor with other works
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances of the PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe for the determination of cefoperazone were compared with the reported performances of other systems. The present nano-optosensor exhibited a good linear range and lower limit of detection which enabled the detection of cefoperazone at ultra-trace levels (Table 2 ). Recovery was comparable or better than other works. The proposed method exhibited good precision with RSDs that were acceptable and better than reported for some other methods. This comparison indicated that the nano-optosensor based on the composite PGr/CdTe QDs/Fe3O4@SiO2/MIP probe was ultra-sensitive toward cefoperazone. In addition, the proposed nano-optosensor was more economical in cost and time than HPLC. Therefore, the proposed nano-optosensor offers an alternative, accurate strategy for trace cefoperazone detection that is ultrasensitive and highly selective.

A magnetic nanocomposite comprising porous graphene, cadmium telluride quantum dots, silica-embedded magnetite and molecularly imprinted polymer was successfully synthesized. The nanocomposite was used in an optosensing system for cefoperazone detection. The excellent optical properties of cadmium telluride quantum dots, the good selectivity of MIP and the high affinity of porous graphene produced an optosensor of high sensitivity and selectivity and enabled a simple and rapid procedure for trace cefoperazone detection. In addition, incorporating silica-embedded magnetite to pre-concentrate the analyte improved the sensitivity of the method, which could detect ultra-trace levels of cefoperazone. The fabricated optosensor detected cefoperazone in milk with good accuracy (recoveries of 85.3–98.3%). The obtained results of the developed nano-optosensor agreed well with the results obtained with high-performance liquid chromatography. Moreover, the developed nano-optosensor can be fabricated with a convenient and rapid procedure and low-cost equipment.

Nuntanut Chaitong: Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft. Nutnicha Chansud: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Naphatsakorn Orachorn: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Warakorn Limbut: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT5-RSA63022-03). The authors would like to acknowledge the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106838.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
